  5. Stay Housed Anaheim Program


#  Stay Housed Anaheim Program 
The Stay Housed Anaheim program promotes housing stability for Anaheim renters by offering one-time emergency rent _or_ security deposit assistance up to $3,000. Utility assistance may be considered up to $500 with a rental assistance request.
The Stay Housed Anaheim Program is now accepting applications [here](https://housingportal.anaheim.net/HousingStability).
Applicants must review the eligibility requirements and submit all required documentation below for review.
Submitting an application does not guarantee assistance. Additional documentation or verification may be requested upon review of application to determine eligibility. Landlord or utility provider participation is required. If an application is approved, payments are made directly to a landlord or utility provider.
Funding for this program is limited. Applications will be accepted online on a first-come, first-served basis as funding is available.
This program may close at any time.
[![Apply for Assistance Button - HSP Opens in new window](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=68216)](https://housingportal.anaheim.net/HousingStability)
### Eligibility Requirements:
  * Anaheim renter for the past 12 consecutive months (owners, subleases and residents in unincorporated Anaheim are not eligible for this program). Anaheim tenants enrolled in a housing program or receiving housing assistance are not eligible for this program.
  * At risk of housing instability or facing a destabilizing event
  * Demonstrate ability to maintain housing beyond assistance period
  * Household income at 50 percent of Orange County area median income or lower

**Household Size** | **1** | **2** | **3** | **4** | **5** | **6** | **7** | **8**  
---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---  
**Maximum Income** | $59,250 | $67,700 | $76,150 | $84,600 | $91,400 | $98,150 | $104,950 | $111,700  
  

### Required Program Documentation:
  * Proof of residency in city of Anaheim
    * Current and complete lease agreement 
    * Anaheim Public Utilities bill
  * Documentation of:
    * Rent and/or utilities owed, such as a rent ledger, utility bill, etc.
    * Security deposit assistance, current and complete lease agreement showing unit information and security deposit amount
  * Income documentation for all household members 18 years or older  



*Additional documentation or verification may be requested upon review of application.

  6. [About Water Quality](https://www.anaheim.net/2569/About-Water-Quality)
  7. Water Hardness


#  Water Hardness 
Most of Southern California, including Anaheim, has hard water. Water is considered “hard” if it contains relatively high levels of calcium and magnesium. These 2 naturally occurring minerals are not a health concern, and in fact, hard water actually supplements your body’s need for these vital nutrients. The hardness of Anaheim’s water is about 300 parts per million, or about 18 grains per gallon, enough calcium to provide up to 10% of the Recommended Daily Allowance by drinking 1 liter of tap water.
Many people prefer water with fewer minerals because hard water leaves mineral deposits on pipes, appliances and dishes and requires more soap when cleaning. Those who prefer to have fewer minerals in their water can install a water softening system in their home or business. There are several types of water softening treatment systems available, so you need to do your homework to determine the best fit for your needs. Anaheim does not endorse any particular type of water softener and has no prohibitions against salt-based systems.
## Types of Softeners
  

### Salt-Based Ion Exchange Systems
  

These systems exchange sodium ions for calcium and magnesium. They add small amounts of sodium to the water, but at levels that are generally not a health issue for most people; however, the softener may be connected to just the hot water influent, if preferred. These systems require frequent additions of salt (one 50-pound bag lasts about 2 to 4 weeks), but otherwise require minimal maintenance. 
  

### Reverse Osmosis Systems
  

These systems use membranes to filter minerals and other contaminants from water. They remove calcium and magnesium as well as chlorine and other possible substances in water. They do not add sodium. Generally, reverse osmosis systems require more maintenance than ion exchange systems and some vendors offer a maintenance program to simplify the process for owners. 
  

### Magnetic Systems
  

These systems apply an electromagnetic charge to incoming water, which causes the water molecules to behave differently. In essence, the water acts as if it were soft water even though no change in mineral concentration has occurred. These systems are relatively new to the market, but they reportedly require very little maintenance. 
  

## Treatment Devices
  

There are a number of commercial entities that are in the business of selling water treatment products. As such, they often claim that water from your local water system is unsatisfactory. All consumers should know that Anaheim tap water (and water from any reputable water utility) is perfectly safe to drink.
In fact, Anaheim’s water meets all state and federal standards established for human health**.** **Any inferences that Anaheim tap water is unsafe, particularly from a water treatment vendor, should be taken as a sales ploy and not as fact.**
Anaheim Public Utilities does not endorse any water treatment method, product, or vendor. If you use a home water treatment system, you should ensure that it is both energy and water efficient.

  7. Lead & Anaheim Water


#  Lead & Anaheim Water 
The water issues in Flint, Michigan in 2016 raised awareness of lead in drinking water. The lead issue in Flint, Michigan’s drinking water was a result of not controlling corrosive water and old house plumbing containing lead pipes. Anaheim has been regularly testing water within customer’s homes since 1993 in adherence with the [Lead & Copper Rule](https://www.epa.gov/dwreginfo/lead-and-copper-rule), one of the Environmental Protection Agency’s National Primary Drinking Water Regulations.
## Anaheim Has No Known Lead Service Lines
Anaheim completed a Lead Service Line Inventory required by U.S. EPA’s Lead and Copper Rule Revisions. By reviewing historical records and conducting field investigations, **Anaheim has determined it has no lead or galvanized requiring replacement service lines in its distribution system.** This includes any privately-owned or customer-owned service lines. This determination was based on the following actions as approved by the State Water Resources Control Board, Division of Drinking Water:
  * Review of available construction and plumbing codes,
  * Review of water system records, such as maps and drawings, sampling results, and meter installation records,
  * Inspections conducted during the course of normal operations, and
  * Representative random field sampling of over 3,000 service lines in Anaheim’s distribution system.


We pride ourselves on meeting all the regulatory requirements for lead in our drinking water system.
  * Water samples collected from various locations in Anaheim are tested every three years by an independent laboratory. All results remain in compliance with the Lead & Copper Rule.
  * Anaheim’s water supply does not contain lead and is non-corrosive.
  * Anaheim does not have any lead pipes in its distribution system. 


For more information on Anaheim’s water, view our [Water Quality Report](https://www.anaheim.net/2092/Water-Quality-Report).
### Volunteer to Sample your Water
You may volunteer to be a water quality sample site for the next round of lead testing in 2024 by emailing waterquality@anaheim.net. If you have immediate concerns about lead in your drinking water and would like a water quality test, you may call us at 714-765-4556.

#  First-Time Homebuyer Program 
## **Program Overview**
Anaheim's First Time Homebuyer program help low- to moderate-income households achieve homeownership by bridging the gap when prices and down payments would otherwise be out of reach. 
##  **“NEW OPPORTUNITY”** My Anaheim Home
**The program offers a $50,000 loan to help with a down payment on a home.**  
It’s a 30-year deferred loan with no monthly payments, charged at 3% simple interest. Buyers must contribute at least 3% of the purchase price, and up to 5% of the down payment can come from family gifts. Total down payment assistance can cover up to 20% of the purchase price.
**To qualify, applicants must:**
  * Meet the income limits
  * Not have owned a home in the last 3 years
  * Buy a home within Anaheim (not unincorporated areas)
  * Live in the home as an owner-occupant
  * Choose an eligible property type (single-family, townhouse, or condo)
  * Stay within the maximum purchase price allowed
  * Complete an approved homebuyer seminar
  * Priority is given to people who live or work in Anaheim for the past 12 months**[![First-Time Homebuyer Interest List Portal Opens in new window](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=67479)](https://housingportal.anaheim.net/Fthb)**


[![My Anaheim Home Program Flyer Image English Opens in new window](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=67314)](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/67312/My-Anaheim-Home-Program-Flyer)
Click on image to download flyer.

  8. Types of Home Water Treatment Devices


#  Types of Home Water Treatment Devices 
Still Interested in Home Water Treatment?
Anaheim Public Utilities realizes that customers’ water preferences vary greatly by individual. Therefore, although our tap water is already completely safe to drink, you may still want to purchase a home water treatment device based on your personal preferences.
If you are considering a home water treatment device purchase, you should educate yourself on the wide variety of choices available on the market today. By knowing what the different home water treatment devices do, you will be able to purchase the right one for you. Provided below is a brief description of the most common options available:
  
Filters
The majority of the filtration systems on the market today use granular activated carbon to treat water coming from a single faucet. Some filters must be filled manually, such as a pitcher, while others, such as faucet filters and under-sink systems, are actually attached directly to the plumbing. Depending on the design and filter media used in the unit, filters are able to reduce many types of substances, such as chlorine and lead.  
  

Water Softeners
Water softeners reduce the level of hardness in water by exchanging calcium and magnesium for sodium and potassium. Water softener units are usually made to treat water for the whole house.  
  

Reverse Osmosis Systems
Reverse osmosis systems work by using pressure to force water through a membrane that reduces minerals including sodium chloride and heavy metals such as copper, chromium, and lead. In addition, some of these systems can also reduce substances such as arsenic, fluoride, and nitrates. Reverse osmosis systems treat water very slowly. Therefore, they usually have a pressurized storage tank to store water to meet household demand.
  
Ultraviolet Treatment Systems
Ultraviolet (UV) treatment systems use ultraviolet light to kill microorganisms such as bacteria and/or viruses. UV system efficiency is affected by the clarity of the incoming water supply. Also, keep in mind that Anaheim Public Utilities already maintains a chlorine residual in its tap water to kill bacteria and microorganisms. Nonetheless, if you have or are considering purchasing a UV system, its efficiency will not be affected by Anaheim tap water since it is neither turbid nor dirty.

  3. [Convention, Sports & Entertainment](https://www.anaheim.net/2595/Convention-Sports-Entertainment)
  4. Visit Anaheim Subcontractor Agreements Approved by the Executive Director of Convention, Sports, and Entertainment 


#  Visit Anaheim Subcontractor Agreements Approved by the Executive Director of Convention, Sports, and Entertainment 
Per the Amended and Restated Agreement with Visit Anaheim, effective June 13, 2025, Visit Anaheim will request and secure the City’s written consent to enter into any contract with any subcontractor for services if the subcontract amount exceeds $150,000 either in an individual contract amount or in an aggregate amount paid to a subcontractor as calculated on annual calendar year basis. Effective November 2025, the award of professional consultant contracts shall be published on the City’s website after an agreement is finalized and are displayed below for a period of 30 days after award. Persons desiring more information about these contracts should contact Tarisa Calato (714) 765-8983. 
* * *
# **FEBRUARY 2026**
### [Tiger House Films](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/70091/Tiger-House-Agreement)
  * Develop and produce a comprehensive suite of high-quality brand storytelling assets that serve as a cornerstone of Visit Anaheim's brand relaunch and the public expression of the destination's new identity. The investment delivers the core creative platform that powers Visit Anaheim’s brand relaunch and supports ongoing destination marketing by providing a scalable, multi-year content library.  
**Award Date:** February 2, 2026  
**Award Amount:** $333,000.00


# **JANUARY 2026**
### [Tourism Economics](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/69766/TE-EI-AND-VISITOR-FORECAST-ANALYTICS-AGRMT-126202670)
  * The contractor will develop an Anaheim Economic Impact and Forecast report. The report covers various essential factors, including the total visitor volume and spending by category, the impact on employment and household income, and the federal, state and local tax contributions stemming from visitor activities.   
**Award Date:** January 20, 2026  
**Award Amount:** $92,500.00  
Note: Tourism Economics has existing agreements with Visit Anaheim. The aggregate amount, including this agreement, exceeds $150,000 and is required to be posted on city's website. 


#  **DECEMBER 2025**  
None
# **NOVEMBER 2025**
### [Rowe Creative Union](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/67562/VA-ROWE-SUB-AGRMNT-NOV-7-2025)
  * ROWE will lead the creation and activation of a fully integrated marketing campaign that builds upon the newly established brand identity. In partnership with Visit Anaheim, ROWE will identify key assets from the comprehensive brand library to prioritize for campaign development and rollout, providing direction and templates for further application.   
**Award Date:** November 10, 2025  
**Award Amount:** $150,000.00


# **OCTOBER 2025**
### [**Miles (Leisure Campaigns)**](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/67260/Miles-Partnership-Agreement_October-2025)
  * Enhance upper-funnel awareness in San Diego and Palm Springs through print and digital ads that highlight the Secret Savings Season promotion.  
**Award Date:** October 8, 2025  
**Award Amount:** $160,000.00

  6. Business Resource Guide


#  Business Resource Guide 
As a community-owned, not-for-profit utility, Anaheim Public Utilities (APU) is dedicated to providing high-quality electric and water services at some of the most competitive rates in Orange County.
APU’s electric grid is approximately 65% underground with an ongoing conversion program to place overhead lines in major roadways underground. Reliable service is provided through 14 substations and high-speed communications. For very large, critical business operations, customers may invest in emergency circuits to minimize downtime.
Anaheim’s water system has high reliability and fire flows that support Anaheim Fire & Rescue’s Class 1 Insurance Services Office (ISO) rating. The Orange County groundwater basin provides the majority of APU’s water, and imported water provides the balance. Having both resources provides reliability and operational flexibility.
To learn about APU’s key performance metrics, visit [Anaheim.net/USI](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/6234/Utilities-Success-Indicators).
![](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=70157)
## AFFORDABLE UTILITY SERVICE
  

### [COST-BASED RATES](https://www.anaheim.net/2003/Rates-Rules-Regulations)
APU offers cost-based rates that help businesses with affordable utility services. Rates recover the cost to operate, maintain, and invest in infrastructure. And for new electric customers or those seeking to expand their enterprise by 200 kilowatts (kW), APU provides an economic development rate that includes discounts to help you grow.
### [REBATES AND EFFICIENCY PROGRAMS](https://www.anaheim.net/2533/Commercial)
To help optimize your operations, our efficiency specialists offer expert guidance and personalized recommendations at no cost. Whether it’s upgrading to high-efficiency equipment or transitioning to drought-tolerant landscaping, we can help you identify improvements that reduce operating costs and extend equipment life.
"The Anaheim Public Utilities team provided valuable guidance throughout the process to improve building performance and helped facilitate the incentive opportunities that made these improvements possible."  
- JW MARRIOTT  
  
---  
###   

### AWARDS AND GRANTS
APU has earned industry recognitions and secured grants, demonstrating our commitment to cost-effectively serving our customers:
  * American Public Power Association (APPA) Safety Award of Excellence
  * APPA Reliable Public Power Provider Platinum designation
  * American Public Works Association So Cal Chapter 2025 BEST Project of the Year for groundwater treatment
  * $10M State Water Resources Control Board Division of Financial Assistance for groundwater treatment
  * $7M FEMA Hazard Mitigation Grant Program for undergrounding wildfire threat zones
  * $7M California Energy Commission’s Community Energy Reliability and Resilience Investment to accelerate replacement of distribution switches


![](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=70158)
## SAFETY & RELIABILITY
  

### [WILDFIRE MITIGATION](https://www.anaheim.net/5487/Wildfire-Mitigation-Plan)
Electric and water systems in high Fire Threat Zones are hardened, with 98% of electric lines already underground, as well as hydrants inspected and tested regularly. We work closely with public safety teams to continue making important investments, while addressing citywide safety, reliability and affordability.
### CAPITAL INVESTMENTS 
With more than 750 miles of water mains and 1,200 miles of electric lines, APU’s capital improvement program aims to enhance service reliability and replace aging infrastructure. This includes pipeline, hydrant, and valve replacements; as well as significant, ongoing investments in electric grid reliability, such as transformer replacements and undergrounding power lines.
### TECHNOLOGY SYSTEMS 
APU owns 136 miles of fiber optic lines to manage its critical electric and water systems, allowing fast detection and response to outages and the ability to re-route power through remote operations. Dark fiber is available for businesses to lease for high-speed internet access and other broadband services. 
![](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=70159)
## SUSTAINABILITY OPTIONS
  

### [GREEN POWER](https://www.anaheim.net/6118/Green-Power)
This rate option is available to customers seeking to reduce their carbon footprint. APU offers higher levels of renewable energy that may be purchased at an additional cost to achieve 60% or 100% green power to cover customer loads.
### [ELECTRIFICATION](https://www.anaheim.net/6333/Commercial-EV-Programs)
APU provides no-cost assessments for electric fleet and building electrification. The assessment is an effective tool to plan out investments in electric infrastructure.
### [REGIONALLY RECYCLED WATER](https://www.anaheim.net/5817/Groundwater-Treatment-Program)
Anaheim pumps from the reliable OC Groundwater Basin—managed by Orange County Water District—that is recharged from the Santa Ana River and supplemented by recycled water to provide a sustainable, drought-proof water source.
### [REPLACING LAWNS](https://www.anaheim.net/6639/Turf-Replacement-Program-for-Commercial-)
APU assists businesses seeking to replace their turf with drought-tolerant landscaping with design assistance and rebates.
"In our family’s more than 60-year history of commercial real estate development and management, we have rarely encountered a team as proactive, responsive, and effective as the staff at Anaheim Public Utilities."  
- Le Baron Investments/Vista Paint Corp  
  
---

#  Service Clubs 
### Anaheim features wide range of service clubs that support our community through volunteerism, outreach, and civic engagement. These organizations play an important role in enhancing the quality of life for residents and strengthening connections across the city. For additional details or to get involved, please contact the organizations directly using the information provided.

#  Planning & Development Section 
## **Services Provided**
The Planning & Development Section of the Community Risk Reduction Division completes reviews and inspections for new construction and fire protection plans. Our role is to ensure projects meet fire and life safety codes in order to support safe building design and construction throughout our community.
## **Permits**
Fire construction permits are required for specific construction and fire protection activities to ensure compliance with fire and life safety codes. For more information, please visit our [Fire Construction Permits](https://www.anaheim.net/6759/Fire-Construction-Permits) page.
## **Documents**
  * [Fire Protection Over the Counter Plan Review](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/51038/Fire-Protection-Over-the-Counter-Plan-Review)
  * [Fee Schedule](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/1268/Life-Safety-Fee-Schedule)
  * [Construction Plan Submittal Information](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/30702/Construction-Plan-Submittal-Info)
  * [Fire Protection Company List](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/17775/Fire-Protection-Contractors-)


## **Disneyland Resort**
Visit [DisneylandForward](https://www.anaheim.net/5961/DisneylandForward) for more development information.

8 a.m. - 5 p.m.

#  2026 State of the City 
![Graphic with Mayor Ashleigh Aitken and date, time and location for the 2026 State of the City](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=69690)
[![Click here button for tables Opens in new window](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=70067)](https://www.eventbrite.com/e/2026-state-of-the-city-luncheon-featuring-mayor-ashleigh-aitken-tickets-1984513838312?utm-campaign=social&utm-content=attendeeshare&utm-medium=discovery&utm-term=listing&utm-source=cp&aff=ebdsshcopyurl)
**Anaheim Mayor Ashleigh Aitken** delivers her 2026 State of the City Address on April 29 at the Grove of Anaheim.
  

An Anaheim tradition, the State of the City address is a chance for our mayor to bring together the community, local nonprofits and businesses to hear an update on all things Anaheim!
  

Always uplifting and entertaining, this year's State of the City will include some special guests.
  

Businesses, nonprofits and anyone interested in attending this fundraising event can buy a ticket, table or sponsor a community table.
  

Anaheim volunteers, community leaders and students are invited to enter an opportunity drawing to receive a free ticket to the event. **Submit your name below by April 15** for a chance to attend this year’s event, those selected will be **notified by April 20.**
  

[![community drawing Opens in new window](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=70137)](https://www.anaheim.net/FormCenter/City-Council-16/2026-State-of-the-City-Address-Community-415)
Proceeds from this nonprofit event touch the lives of many in our community through the Anaheim Community Foundation.
  

Can’t attend? The event will be livestreamed here at Anaheim.net/StateoftheCity.

  7. Substation Improvement


#  Substation Improvement 
Substations are a critical part of the local grid as they distribute electricity to area residents and businesses. Anaheim Public Utilities relies on operational flexibility and maintenance of infrastructure to preserve the integrity of our electric system, which reduces the duration of power outages affecting areas throughout the city. 
As part of APU's ongoing capital project improvements, substation improvements to replace aging equipment maximize system performance and resiliency, alleviate congestion on existing infrastructure, and ensure long-term reliability for all Anaheim customers.
  1. [Sharp Substation](https://www.anaheim.net/6756/Substation-Improvement#tab0135b244-b1fc-481b-a905-78bc28aea3dc_0)


**Project Timeline**
Phase 1: Fall 2023 – Spring 2025  
Phase 2: Fall 2027 – Spring 2029
To maintain continuous, safe, and reliable service, while utilizing the existing limited space on site, the Sharp Substation Upgrade Project has been split into two (2) separate phases. This approach allows crews to upgrade one side of the substation at a time while keeping the other side in service, avoiding impacts to customers. 
The first phase of the project has been completed, which has replaced half of the substation equipment nearing the end of its life. The second phase of the project will mirror the first phase to complete the substation upgrade. 
  1. [Southwest Substation](https://www.anaheim.net/6756/Substation-Improvement#tab0135b244-b1fc-481b-a905-78bc28aea3dc_1)


**Project Timeline**
Design: Winter 2024 – Winter 2025   
Construction: Fall 2025 – Summer 2027 
As electrification increases power demand citywide, the Southwest Substation upgrade will replace aging utility equipment by installing new equipment on a site adjacent to the current substation. The new site's location allows construction to occur without interrupting existing service. 
The project's improvements offer grid modernization, capacity upgrades, and operational flexibility during outages. 
  1. [Vermont Substation](https://www.anaheim.net/6756/Substation-Improvement#tab0135b244-b1fc-481b-a905-78bc28aea3dc_2)


**Project Timeline**
Project Award: Spring 2026  
Design: Spring 2026 – Fall 2027   
Construction: Fall 2027 – Summer 2029 
This will be the first installation of its kind in the United States. The planned expansion to Vermont Substation will help maintain reliable power to residents and local businesses and minimize strain on our existing power grid.
APU is committed to lowering our carbon footprint and prioritizing green energy. The newly installed Gas Insulated Equipment (GIE) will be “vacuum” technology, which has a Global Warming Impact of zero and 100% California Air Resources Board (CARB) compliant.
![substation improvement](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=68157)
![substation-02](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=68158)
[![Public Utilities Home Page](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=42943)](https://www.anaheim.net/6099)

  1. [Home](https://www.anaheim.net/)
  2. [Departments](https://www.anaheim.net/148/Departments)
  3. [Housing and Community Development](https://www.anaheim.net/6363/Housing-and-Community-Development)
  4. [Initiatives](https://www.anaheim.net/6384/Initiatives)
  5. [Anaheim Local Housing Trust Fund](https://www.anaheim.net/6693/Anaheim-Local-Housing-Trust-Fund)
  6. About Anaheim's Local Housing Trust Fund


![Logo with dept tagline 8-7-25](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=67311)
The Anaheim Local Housing Trust Fund was created in August 2024 to help finance affordable communities, provide down payments for first-time homebuyers and help those at risk of eviction. 
The fund is designed to receive and invest both public and private funding to support housing in Anaheim. This may include in-leiu fees, state housing grants, federal housing grants and donations from philanthropic partners. 
At the time the ordinance was adopted, the city expected a major initial contribution from the Walt Disney Company. They committed $15 million to the fund in the summer of 2025, with an additional $15 million expected in five years.
The trust fund is meant to help expand affordable housing and support housing programs that serve Anaheim residents. To guide how the funds will be used, our City Council approved program guidelines and funding allocations in May 2025.
Click [here](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/66427/ADOPTED-Anaheim-Local-Housing-Trust-Fund-Program-Guidelines-and-Funding-Allocation-Plan?bidId=) to view the approved program guidelines and funding allocation.
The plan outlines three main areas of investment.
  * Financing affordable housing communities
  * Providing down payment assistance for first-time homebuyers
  * Supporting programs that promote housing stability for Anaheim renters 


Find more information on each program below.
  * [ BUILD MORE HOMES INITIATIVE ](https://www.anaheim.net/6697/Build-More-Homes-Initiative)


  * [ FIRST-TIME HOMEBUYER PROGRAM ](https://www.anaheim.net/6695)


  * [ STAY HOUSED ANAHEIM PROGRAM ](https://www.anaheim.net/6696)


![ALHTF Programs Photo](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=69728)

  5. [Get a Permit](https://www.anaheim.net/6757/Get-a-Permit)
  6. Electronic Plan Review Information


#  Electronic Plan Review Information 
## **Electronic Plan Review Information**
  1. **Create a GoPost login, if you don’t already have one:**
    1. [GoPost online portal](https://anaheim.eplansoftreview.com/#/login?portal=AnaheimFire)
    2. [Walkthrough Video Guide](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EeDPsGL2tUQ)
  2. **Project Submission & Payment**
    1. For new submittals, click the green plus sign in the lower right corner.
      1. Fill out project information and upload plans, [Plan Review Application](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/54379/Fire-Construction-Permit-Application----Online-Fillable?bidId=), and any additional supporting documents.
         * Ensure the application is complete and that the contractor’s license, workers’ comp., and Anaheim business license are active to speed up processing and reduce the delay of project acceptance.
      2. Click on the green SUBMIT button in the top right corner to complete the submission.
    2. For resubmittals, locate existing project on your dashboard and click on three vertical dots, then click “Edit.”
      1. Upload revised plans and any additional documents as needed.
      2. Click on green SUBMIT button on top right corner to complete submission.
    3. Please allow 2-3 business days for our Fire Development staff to review the submission. If submission is deemed complete, you will receive instructions on how to make the payment.
      1. Payments can be made online with a Visa, Master Card or Discover card (No American Express) through our [Anaheim Next](https://aca-prod.accela.com/ANAHEIM/Default.aspx) portal or by check made payable to: City of Anaheim
    4. After receiving payment, the project will be processed for plan check review.
  3. **Plan Review**
    1. Please allow up to 30 business days for initial plan reviews or up to 15 business days for resubmittal plan reviews.
    2. To request an expedited plan review, please email Assistant Fire Marshal David Rodriguez at DRodriguez4@anaheim.net and include FPI#. If approved, he will notify the Fire Development team to expedite the submittal.
  4. **Corrections**
    1. You will receive an email notification if the plans require corrections.
    2. Corrections package may be downloaded by opening the project on GoPost and clicking on the Deliverables tab.
  5. **Approval & Permit Issuance**
    1. You will receive an automatic system notification once your project is approved. Please contact our Fire Development team at (714) 765-4000 to schedule an appointment to pick up your issued permit.
    2. Stamped approved plans will be emailed at the time of permit pickup.


If you require further assistance or have any questions, please call our Fire Development team at (714) 765-4000.

#  Operations & Support Services 
The Deputy Chief oversees the Operations and Support Services of the department and assists our Fire Chief in the daily management and administration of all aspects of the Fire Department's operations and activities, as well as acting for the Chief in his absence.

#  Planning Services Division 
We are here to provide a quality environment for Anaheim's residents, businesses and visitors, through the thoughtful implementation of our [general plan](https://www.anaheim.net/712/General-Plan) and [zoning code](https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/anaheim/latest/anaheim_ca/0-0-0-13931).
[![Image and link to Anaheim NEXT ](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=49747)](https://aca-prod.accela.com/anaheim/default.aspx)
[![EPR Button](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=35415)](https://anaheim.net/879/)
## Electronic Plan Review
[Discretionary Permit](https://www.anaheim.net/5755/) and [Administrative Permit](https://www.anaheim.net/5756/)[ _GoPost_ online portal](https://anaheim.eplansoftreview.com/#/login?portal=AnaheimPlanning). By using [GoPost](https://anaheim.eplansoftreview.com/#/login?portal=AnaheimPlanning) you can submit applications online to the Planning Division and upload all plans and supporting documents. Application materials may also be submitted in-person by appointment at the Planning Services Division Counter at City Hall. Digital format of application materials are required for in-person submittal. Contact Eibet Olmedo at EOlmedo@anaheim.net or 714-765-5139. All planning application and filing deadline information can be found on the [Forms & Applications website](https://www.anaheim.net/879/).
Counter Hours
Monday - Friday
8 a.m. - 5 p.m.
  

Phone Hours
Monday - Friday
8 a.m. - 5 p.m.

## Accessory Dwelling Units & Junior Accessory Dwelling Units
The City Council continues to update applicable development standards as new State laws become effective. The City of Anaheim's latest Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) and Junior Accessory Dwelling Unit (JADU) development standards are accessible via the City's [municipal code](https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/anaheim/latest/anaheim_ca/0-0-0-67963).
  * For developing standard ADUs, review the Planning Services Division's [Accessory Dwelling Unit Submittal Requirements Checklist](https://anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/35053/) and visit [Anaheim.net/Building](https://Anaheim.net/Building) for Building Division's Plan Check Submittal requirements. 
  * For more information on ADU development in Anaheim, visit [Anaheim.net/ADUheim](http://www.anaheim.net/ADUheim). 


  

  * [ADU Delay of Enforcement Application (Assembly Bill 2533 and Senate Bill 13)](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/58139/ADU-Delay-of-Enforcement-Application-Assembly-Bill-2533-and-Senate-Bill-13)


## Senate Bill No. 9
The City Council adopted a new ordinance addressing Senate Bill No. 9 on January 25, 2022. Ordinance No. 6524 became effective on February 24, 2022 and provides development standards for a Two Unit Development and Urban Lot Splits. 
  * Review Ordinance 6524 by clicking [here](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/41682/Ordinance-6524). 
    * Development standards are also accessible via the City's [municipal code](https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/anaheim/latest/anaheim_ca/0-0-0-80296). 


## Ordinance 6555
The City Council adopted Ordinance 6555 on April 4, 2023. This ordinance included amendments to various Chapters of Title 15 (Buildings and Housing) and Title 18 (Zoning) of the Anaheim Municipal Code.
Ordinance 6555 became effective on Thursday May 4, 2023. 
Review Ordinance 6555 by clicking [here](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/49055/Ordinance-6555).

  6. Well Destruction


#  Well Destruction 
Improperly abandoned wells have the potential to allow near-surface contaminants to migrate deeper into high-quality drinking water aquifers. Once a drinking water aquifer is contaminated, it is extremely expensive to clean it up. Therefore, Anaheim, which relies heavily on the groundwater basin for its water supply, has taken a number of actions to help prevent abandoned wells from negatively impacting groundwater quality.
  * A well permit program has been established to track all well installations. When wells are no longer needed for their intended purpose, the well owner is required to properly destroy the well. Access the [well permit webpage](https://www.anaheim.net/795/Well-Permits).
  * The city’s public nuisance abatement procedures allow the city to destroy an abandoned well and place a lien on the property to recover abatement costs.
  * Two groundwater protection grants have been obtained by the Public Utilities Department to destroy abandoned wells where no responsible party could be located. In total, 18 wells were destroyed using state funding through these 2 grants.


If any resident is **aware of an abandoned well in Anaheim,** please notify the environmental specialist at 714-765-4166 or email the specialist.

  6. Storage Tank Cleanup


#  Underground Storage Tank Cleanup Program 
Underground storage tank (UST) regulations require cleanup of contamination resulting from UST leaks. As part of its Groundwater Protection Program, the City of Anaheim managed the UST Cleanup Oversight Program from April 1991 to June 2014. However, due to changes in state law, all UST cleanup cases in Anaheim are now managed by the California Regional Water Quality Control Board, Santa Ana Region.
  

Records of UST cleanups may be accessed in several ways. The State of California maintains a database called [Geotracker](http://geotracker.waterboards.ca.gov/%20), which has all known UST cleanup cases in California. The database is searchable by city and address. Anaheim uploaded into the Geotracker database all no further action (NFA) letters and case summaries that it had available in its files. In some cases, NFA letters and/or case summaries were not available for cases that were closed prior to 1991.
  

UST cleanup records may also be accessed from Anaheim Public Utilities by contacting via email or by calling 714-765-4288. If records other than an NFA letter and/or case summary are required, a [records request form](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/1849/Public-Utilities-Records-Request) is required. The [Regional Water Quality Control Board website](http://www.waterboards.ca.gov/santaana/water_issues/programs/ust/index.shtml) provides information on accessing information on UST cases they manage. 
## When is Cleanup Considered Adequate
  

It should be noted that in some cases, a site may be considered adequately cleaned up to protect groundwater and current land use even though some contamination is still present. However, there is the possibility that if there is a change in land use, the residual contamination could pose a threat to future occupants of the site. Therefore, it is always prudent to investigate the site to determine if additional work would be required for a proposed change in land use. Anaheim staff are available to provide assistance with these sorts of inquiries. 
  

One final note, Anaheim Fire and Rescue, available by calling 714-765-4040, regulates the underground storage of hazardous materials, including issuing permits and overseeing the removal of USTs. In cases where tanks are removed and no contamination is found, Anaheim Fire and Rescue issues a no further action letter and the site is not included on the City of Anaheim [UST cleanup case list](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/1336).

  5. Advanced Provider Response Unit (APRU) 


#  Advanced Provider Response Unit (APRU) 
The Advanced Provider Response Unit (APRU) is a Mobile Integrated Health program developed by Anaheim Fire & Rescue that goes beyond the traditional 911 response. The APRU pairs a highly trained Nurse Practitioner with Anaheim Fire paramedics to follow up after critical incidents such as overdoses, behavioral health crises, and other calls where additional support may be needed after emergency crews leave the scene.
Rather than waiting for the next emergency, the APRU reaches out to residents following a crisis. The team works with individuals facing challenges such as addiction, mental health concerns, homelessness, or medical needs that make it difficult to remain stable and healthy. These situations affect not only the individual, but also families, neighborhoods, businesses, and the community as a whole.
By connecting with residents soon after an incident, the APRU helps break the cycle of repeated emergencies. The team provides basic medical care, evaluates ongoing health needs, and may offer appropriate medications to support recovery when needed. The APRU also helps remove common barriers by guiding individuals through available services, assisting with transportation needs, and helping them understand the next steps toward getting help.
A key part of the program is connecting residents with the right resources. The APRU works closely with behavioral health providers, treatment programs, housing services, and community organizations to help individuals move toward stability and improved quality of life. Every person is treated with dignity and respect, with the goal of helping them find long-term support.
The mission of the Advanced Provider Response Unit is to improve outcomes for individuals while strengthening the overall well-being of the Anaheim community. By helping vulnerable residents get the care and support they need, the program reduces repeat emergency calls, lessens the strain on hospitals and public safety resources, and contributes to safer neighborhoods.
Anaheim Fire & Rescue remains committed to innovative programs that support both public safety and community health. The APRU reflects that commitment by ensuring that care continues even after the emergency call is over.

#  Used Oil & Household Hazardous Waste 
The Anaheim Fire & Rescue Hazardous Materials Section and CalRecycle maintain a program that promotes the proper management of used oil and household hazardous waste. Many of the household products we use contain hazardous chemicals that pose a threat to both the public and the environment when they are managed improperly. Household hazardous waste can hurt our environment, family members, trash handlers and landfill workers. If household hazardous waste is poured into household drains or dumped into storm drains, it can upset operations in wastewater treatment plants and cause damage to local waterways and beaches.  
  

## Household Hazardous Waste
  
Chemicals - we use them all the time in our homes such as motor oils, insect spray, bleaches, solvents. Most of us feel that as long as we do not ingest any of these chemicals, the worst that can happen to us is a little skin irritation.  
  
In actuality, these chemicals make up little chemistry sets found in each of our homes: under kitchen sinks, in garages and bathrooms.  
  
Reduce your chances of being harmed by completing the following safety steps:  

  * Read manufacturer labels
  * Store chemicals properly
  * Handle chemicals with care
  * Dispose of chemicals free of charge at our local [Household Hazardous Waste Collection Center](http://oclandfills.com/hazardous) located at 1071 N. Blue Gum Street, in Anaheim. 
  * Anaheim has 31 convenient locations, where you can drop your used oil, please call 714-765-4048 to find a location near you.

For more information, please call 714-834-6752.  
  

## Used Motor Oil
  
After you save yourself some money by conducting oil changes at home, save the earth by recycling the waste oil. To find a local business that will recycle your oil free of charge, please call 800-CLEANUP or [visit the website](https://www.calrecycle.ca.gov/usedoil/).  
  
Once you know where to recycle your used motor oil, you can “Close the Loop” by purchasing re-refined motor oil. Re-refined motor oil is recycled oil that has been processed to make it reusable. It is of the same quality as virgin motor oil. In fact, just ask Mercedes Benz! They have been using re-refined motor oil in their new cars since the early 1990’s. Purchasing re-refined motor oil will:  

  * Reduce air pollution
  * Use an American resource and reduce dependence on foreign oil
  * Reduce energy requirements by being used over and over again
  * Conserve a valuable, non-renewable resource (crude oil) 


## Oil Filter Exchange Events & Used Oil Containers
  
  
  
Kit is available to Anaheim residents and includes:  
  

  * Two shop rags
  * Recycling container
  * Center locations card

#  Be Water Smart - Save Water 
California experiences periods of drought, with the most recent drought lasting three years between 2019 and 2022. APU collaborates with regional partners and adjacent cities to implement long-term solutions by strategically implementing water sustainability initiatives. Expansion of the Orange County Water District’s Groundwater Replenishment System provides access to drought-proof supplies for future resiliency, and APU has ongoing capital projects to restore full access to the local aquifer. 
To remain resilient, we must continue to plan for future drought-related impacts by modernizing water systems and investing in innovative and sustainable projects. Anaheim Public Utilities recognizes we are part of one State with interconnected water supplies and we must be part of the solution for the current statewide dry conditions. We must minimize wasteful water use, including:
  * Washing driveways and sidewalks with water
  * Using water that runs into the street or gutter
  * Use of a hose without a shutoff nozzle


Anaheim Public Utilities currently offers more than **45 rebates and incentive programs** to assist Anaheim businesses, residents, income-qualified customers and schools **save water, energy, and money**. Whether you are making capital improvements, replacing inefficient equipment, or looking for free services on ways to help you save, we have a program that’s right for you!
  * [Residents](https://www.anaheim.net/2532/Residential)
  * [Businesses](https://www.anaheim.net/2533/Commercial)
  * [Educators](https://www.anaheim.net/5361/Schools)


* * *
## Virtual and In-Person Landscaping Workshop Series
Anaheim Public Utilities is pleased to announce a series of landscaping workshops, hosted in partnership with the Metropolitan Water District of Southern California and presented by the Green Gardens Group, a leader in sustainable landscaping*. Online workshops will provide an overview of California-friendly and native landscape training, along with elements of turf removal and garden transformation.**For virtual and regional classes,** please visit the [](https://greengardensgroup.com/turf-transformation/)[**Green Gardens Group page**](https://greengardensgroup.com/turf-transformation/) to view workshop dates and times.**For local Anaheim classes** please check the[**Anaheim Libraries calendar**](https://anaheim.net/calendar.aspx?CID=38,24,52&showPastEvents=false)**.**
_*Registration is required to attend._

  6. Anaheim Landscape Requirements


#  Anaheim Landscape Requirements 
In Southern California, more than half of our urban water supply is used to irrigate landscapes. One of the best ways to conserve our limited resources is to make these landscapes water-efficient.   
  
State law (Assembly Bill 1881) required that cities and counties update their water efficient landscape ordinances by January 1, 2010. In response, the city adopted a landscape water efficiency ordinance as the new Chapter 10.19 of the Anaheim Municipal Code. 
![Kaffir Lily flower](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=1516)
  1. ![Autumn sage flower](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Path?filePath=%2fdocuments%5cIntranet%5c69%5c174%2fAutumn+Sage_201502030921102477.jpg)
  2. ![Bougain villea flower](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Path?filePath=%2fdocuments%5cIntranet%5c69%5c174%2fBougain+Villea_201502030921103259.jpg)
  3. ![Day lily flower](https://www.anaheim.net/723/Anaheim-Landscape-Requirements)
  4. ![Fraser photinia flower](https://www.anaheim.net/723/Anaheim-Landscape-Requirements)
  5. ![Indian Hawthorn bush](https://www.anaheim.net/723/Anaheim-Landscape-Requirements)
  6. ![Little John flower](https://www.anaheim.net/723/Anaheim-Landscape-Requirements)
  7. ![Green plant](https://www.anaheim.net/723/Anaheim-Landscape-Requirements)
  8. ![Trailing latana flower](https://www.anaheim.net/723/Anaheim-Landscape-Requirements)
  9. ![Variegated pittosporum flower](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Path?filePath=%2fdocuments%5cIntranet%5c69%5c174%2fVariegated+Pittosporum_201502030921110759.jpg)


The [Anaheim Guidelines](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/17985) provide landscape design parameters and specifications for residents, landscape professionals, developers, and contractors to follow when planning landscape projects in the city. 
## Landscape Water Efficiency Ordinance & Guidelines
  * [Anaheim Municipal Code (A.M.C.) Landscape Water Efficiency Ordinance](https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/anaheim/latest/anaheim_ca/0-0-0-60381)
  * [Landscape Guidelines Overview](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/17989)
  * [Landscape Water Efficiency Ordinance Guidelines](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/13241/Landscape-Water-Efficiency-Guidelines)


If you have questions on the ordinance, please contact the [Planning Department](https://www.anaheim.net/490/Planning-Building) at 714-765-5139.

](http://www.bewaterwise.com/calculator.html)

  * [](http://www.bewaterwise.com/calculator.html)[](http://www.bewaterwise.com/calculator.html)[Be Water Wise Watering Calculator](http://www.bewaterwise.com/calculator.html)

  5. Financial Services


#  Financial Services 
The Financial Services Section supports Anaheim Fire & Rescue by processing payroll, preparing invoices for inspections and other activities, ordering supplies and equipment, and preparing the annual budget.   
  
Managing Funds Effectively  
This section is responsible for making sure that the funds provided for fire safety are properly accounted for and spent in the most cost effective way possible. The Financial Services team does this by working with the department's other divisions to ensure that Anaheim Fire & Rescue has reliable equipment and highly trained personnel to respond to emergencies.  

## ![RussellNarahara2024](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=53863)
## Russell Narahara
Email
Administrative Services Manager
Monitoring Department Budget  
An important role of this section is to monitor the department's budget. This includes preparing an accurate annual budget, then watching the budget throughout the year. If potential problem areas are discovered, plans are adjusted to meet the needs of the department without going over the budget that City Council approved.  
  
Analysis & Research  
The Financial Services Section also performs business analysis and research to identify best practices and areas for improvement. Team members work closely with other City departments to improve financial operations, and partners with other government agencies to manage large projects that reach beyond the borders of the City to improve the services provided to Anaheim residents, business owners, and visitors.

#  Anaheim Colony 
![Historic District Monument next to the road in front of palm trees](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=1492)
In October 1997, City Council adopted the Anaheim Colony Historic District. This is the city's 1st and largest Historic District. The boundaries of the Colony match the original German Colony founded in 1857 (North, South, East and West Streets). Over 1,000 qualified structures are located within this 1.8-square mile area. On January 26, 1999, City Council approved the Anaheim Colony Monument, in which 4 were installed to mark the main entries of the district, located at:  

  * North Street and Anaheim Boulevard
  * South Street and Anaheim Boulevard
  * East Street and Lincoln Avenue
  * West Street and Lincoln Avenue


![Anaheim Colony map](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=1491)
Wohlgemuth House Cactus  
An interesting feature of the 1916 Wohlgemuth House located at 211 North Olive Street was a 50 year-old cactus planted on the property next to the home. It needed to be removed before restoring the property to its original condition. This enormous specimen was such an incredible size that the City hired a moving specialist who brought in a crane and lifted the 4-ton cactus onto a flat bed. Lincoln Avenue was briefly closed down, and the cactus was slowly and carefully hauled down the streets of the Colony to be planted in Rudy Boysen's cactus garden at Pearson Park where it appears to be doing very well. 
  1. ![211 North Olive house with truck next to the house to remove the cactus](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Path?filePath=%2fdocuments%5cIntranet%5c81%5c168%5c170%2f211+North+Olive+House+With+Cactus_201502030737123727.jpg)
  2. ![211 North Olive house](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Path?filePath=%2fdocuments%5cIntranet%5c81%5c168%5c170%2f211+North+Olive+House_201502030737125914.jpg)
  3. ![Rudy Boysen&#39s cactus garden](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Path?filePath=%2fdocuments%5cIntranet%5c81%5c168%5c170%2fHistoric+Preservation+Colony+Cactus+Garden_201502030737149977.png)

#  Five Points 
In October 2004, the Five Points Historic District was adopted by City Council to preserve a concentration of architecturally unique and significant homes from about the same era as the Anaheim Colony Historic District.  
  
Location  
The Five Points Historic District is the 1st historic district in addition to our original Anaheim Colony Historic District. Its name is derived from the Five Points street intersection at Lincoln, West and Center Streets, where the historic Five Points commercial building was originally built. This building has been moved westerly, completely restored and now home to a Ruby's Deluxe Restaurant and Drive-in; the 14st Ruby's Drive-In Diner of its kind. 
![Ruby's deluxe restaurant and drive-in](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=1495)
![Five Points map](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=1494)

#  Historic Districts 
The City of Anaheim has [4 distinct historic districts](http://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/1509) located in Central Anaheim. These districts encompass approximately 2-square miles.  

[![The 4 historic districts are colored coded on the map Opens in new window](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=1488)](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=1488)
Since 1997, the Anaheim City Council has adopted four historic districts that are located in Central Anaheim. Although Anaheim is comprised of historically significant structures throughout the City's 50 square miles, the vast majority are clustered in the four historic districts shown below. These districts encompass approximately two-square miles of Anaheim.
All of the district's historic homes have been inventoried and many have been surveyed in detail. Certain structures in these districts are eligible to participate in the [Mills Act Program](https://www.anaheim.net/748/) that was approved by City Council in 2000. This program may allow property owners to enjoy a reduction in property tax in exchange for restoring the exteriors of their homes and maintaining them in a historically accurate condition.
  

9
1
1
2
  1. ####  [Anaheim Colony](https://www.anaheim.net/742/Anaheim-Colony)
Anaheim Colony District is the city's 1st and largest Historic District.
  2. ####  [Five Points](https://www.anaheim.net/743/Five-Points)
the Five Points District is to preserve a concentration of architecturally unique and significant homes from about the same era as the Anaheim Colony Historic District.
  3. ####  [Historic Palm](https://www.anaheim.net/744/Historic-Palm)
This Historic Palm District has approximately 180 qualified historic structures that are mostly French, English and Spanish themed.
  4. ####  [Hoskins](https://www.anaheim.net/745/Hoskins)
Hoskins represents a more modern time in Anaheim’s history, an era immediately following WWII.

#  Hoskins 
In December 2008, the City Council adopted the Hoskins Historic District. This is a small neighborhood that was carved from the north end within the original Anaheim Colony Historic District and became its own independent district because it has an intact collection of interesting homes built in the 1940s and 1950s. It represents a more modern time in Anaheim’s history, an era immediately following WWII.  
  
Properties  
There are 53 properties in this district of which 43 are considered to be Qualified Historic Structures.  
  
Ranch Style  
Driving down the streets of this district brings back thoughts of familiar Ranch style houses that were just evolving as homes favored by Anaheim’s middle class community leaders. 
  1. ![Hoskins 720 North Helena house with light post out front](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Path?filePath=%2fdocuments%5cIntranet%5c81%5c168%5c173%2fHoskins+720+North+Helena_201502030816036665.jpg)

![Hoskins map](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=1499)

#  Historic Palm 
In October 2006, City Council adopted the addition of our 3rd district, the Historic Palm District (named after Palm Street, currently known as Harbor Boulevard). This filled the gap between North Street and La Palma Avenue north of the Anaheim Colony Historic District.  
  
This Historic Palm District has approximately 180 qualified historic structures that are mostly French, English and Spanish themed. 
![Historic Palm house with pillars](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=1496)
![Historic Palm District map](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=1497)

#  Support Services 
The Support Services Division:  

  * Provides non-emergency support to the [Operations Division](https://www.anaheim.net/678/Operations)
  * Monitors all capital improvement projects
  * Coordinates facility and apparatus repairs and replacements
  * Processes large equipment purchases
  * Provides for the purchase, storage and inventory of all equipment and expendable supplies needed for the day-to-day operations of the department. 

This division manages the Service Section, Emergency Management and Preparedness Section, and the Department’s Information Technology Section. 
###  ![Picture of Michael Byard in front of an American flag with a grey background behind the flag](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=67337)**Michael Byard**

To view Anaheim properties with Mills Act contracts, click on the Historic District you would like to explore. If you would like additional information on any of the properties, please contact HPreservation@anaheim.net.

#  Anaheim Heritage Center 
## Anaheim Heritage Center ~ Disney Resort Reading Room at the MUZEO
  
Please visit our library in Downtown Anaheim on the promenade. Opened in 1967, the history room contains almost a million items organized and cataloged for use by scholars, students, researchers and community residents. Trained library personnel are available to offer assistance.  
  

### Interests
  
For a specific area of interest, please click on a link below:  

  * Heritage Reading Room
  * [Historic Photographs](https://www.anaheim.net/1294/Anaheim-Photo-Collections)
  * [Historical Society](http://anaheimhistoricalsociety.com/)
  * [Online Catalog](http://ipac.anaheim.net/ipac20/ipac.jsp?profile=z#focus)


![Man working at the library on his laptop](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=1616)

(Hours subject to change)

#  Urban Water Management Plan 
The City of Anaheim (City) would like to invite all interested persons to the public hearing for the City’s 2025 Urban Water Management Plan (2025 UWMP), including the proposed Water Shortage Contingency Plan (WSCP). The public hearing will be held in the 2nd Floor Gordon Hoyt Conference Room of Anaheim West Tower, 201 S. Anaheim Boulevard, on Wednesday, April 22, 2026, at 5:00 p.m., or as soon thereafter as this matter can be heard. The purpose of the hearing is to consider public comments prior to recommending that the City Council adopt the proposed 2025 UWMP, including the proposed WSCP.
The proposed 2025 UWMP and WSCP are prepared in accordance with the California Urban Water Management Planning Act (Act). The Act requires the City, as an urban water supplier, to prepare and adopt an Urban Water Management Plan to analyze water management issues unique to its water service area, including local and imported water supply sources, forecasted water demands, water conservation issues, and water shortage contingency planning. The City is required to update its plan at least once every five years with last adoption by the City Council occurring in 2021.
ALL INTERESTED PERSONS are invited to attend the public hearing and express opinions on the above-described matter. Members of the public can also submit comments electronically for Board Member consideration by sending them to apupubliccomments@anaheim.net prior to the public hearing.
Copies of the proposed 2025 UWMP and WSCP are available for public review on this webpage and at the following locations:
  * Anaheim Central Public Library (500 W. Broadway)
  * Anaheim Canyon Hills Branch Library (400 Scout Trail)
  * East Anaheim Branch Library (8201 E. Santa Ana Canyon Road)
  * Anaheim Euclid Branch (1340 S. Euclid Street)
  * Anaheim Haskett Branch Library (2650 W. Broadway)
  * Anaheim Sunkist Branch (901 S. Sunkist Street)
  * Office of Anaheim City Clerk (Suite #217 Front Counter at 200 S. Anaheim Boulevard)
  * Anaheim West Tower (First Floor Front Counter at 201 S. Anaheim Boulevard)


For more information, please contact David Kim, Principal Civil Engineer, at 714-765-4206 or dkim@anaheim.net.
[Draft Proposed 2025 Urban Water Management Plan](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/70371/Draft-Anaheim-2025-UWMP)
[Draft Proposed 2025 Water Shortage Contingency Plan](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/70372/Draft-Anaheim-2025-WSCP)
[2020 Urban Water Management Plan including the proposed Water Shortage Contingency Plan](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/37199/Anaheim-2020-UWMP_Appx_2)
[Amendments to Anaheim’s 2015 Urban Water Management Plan](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/37198/2015-Amendment---Reduced-Reliance)
[Errata Sheet for Minor Corrections](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/40772/Errata-Sheet-for-Minor-Corrections-for-City-of-Anaheim)

250 East Center Street Anaheim, CA 92805

## Childhood Lead Poisoning Prevention Program (CLPPP)
The CLPPP provides many resources to reduce lead hazards such as case management, investigations, & pipe replacements if eligible.  
[**To learn about CLPPP, please click here**](https://www.ochealthinfo.com/lead)[Flyer](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/49089/CPLLL-Flyer---English-4-2023) | [Folleto en español](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/49090/CPLLL-LHRP-Flyer---Spanish-4-2023) | [Tờ rơi bằng tiếng Việt](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/49091/CPLLL-LHRP-Flyer---Vietnamese-4-2023) | 
## Anaheim Rental Assistance for Older Adults
The City of Anaheim administers the Senior Safety Net (SSN) Program which offers housing stabilization assistance to eligible city of Anaheim residents.  
[**To learn more, please click here**](https://www.anaheim.net/6417/Senior-Safety-Net-Program)  
Phone: (714) 765-4300  
## Source of Income(SOI) Discrimination
The law prohibits discrimination against any applicant or tenant because they are using any type of federal, state, or local housing subsidy to assist with paying rent. This includes but is not limited to, the Section 8 voucher/HCV program.**  
****[SOI CA Civil Rights Dept. FAQ](https://calcivilrights.ca.gov/wp-content/uploads/sites/32/2020/02/SourceofIncomeFAQ_ENG.pdf)**  
**[**HUD SOI Website**](https://www.hud.gov/program_offices/public_indian_housing/programs/hcv/source-of-income-protections) ****  
**If you think you have been a victim of SOI discrimination, please contact the CA Civil Rights Dept.:[**calcivilrights.ca.gov/complaintprocess**](http://calcivilrights.ca.gov/complaintprocess)  
Toll Free: 800.884.1684 TTY: 800.700.2320  | 
## Reporting Housing Discrimination 
If you believe you were a victim of housing discrimination, you have the right to file a housing discrimination complaint with the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD). Because there are time limits on when an allegation can be filed with HUD after an alleged violation, you should report housing discrimination as soon as possible.  
[**To learn how you can report, please CLICK HERE**](https://www.ochealthinfo.com/leadhttps://anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/55667/Reporting-Housing-Discrimination)[Online Form](http://Online) | [Formulario](https://portalapps.hud.gov/FHEO903/Form903/Form903Start.action?lang=es) | [Hình thức](https://portalapps.hud.gov/FHEO903/Form903/Form903Start.action?lang=vi)  
**Know Your Rights as a California Tenant**  
As a tenant in California, you have many important rights. For example, you may be protected from overly high rent increases and certain types of evictions. There are also laws protecting you from discrimination, retaliation, and lockouts where the landlord has not gotten a court order to evict you. To learn more about your rights as a tenant, visit the [**CA Attorney General's Office Landlord-Tenant page**](https://oag.ca.gov/consumers/general/landlord-tenant-issues) or view the flyer [**here**](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/56793/Know-Your-Rights-as-a-California-Tenant).  
|  **OC HMIS Privacy Forms**  
Families currently receiving services through the Orange County HMIS can access**important privacy documents** —including the Consent to Share Protected Personal Information, Privacy Notice, and related forms—by visiting [**ochmis.org/privacy-forms**](https://ochmis.org/privacy-forms)**.**  
  
Specifically, the **OC HMIS Privacy Notice** can be accessed here in [English](https://ochmis.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/05-2025-Privacy-Notice-English.pdf), [Spanish](https://ochmis.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/05-2025_Privacy_Notice_-_English_Spanish.pdf), and [Vietnamese](https://ochmis.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/05-2025_Privacy_Notice_-_English_Vietnamese.pdf).

#  Anaheim Water Sources 
## **Where Does Our Water Come From?**
  
Anaheim’s water supply is a blend of groundwater from our own wells and water imported from Northern California and the Colorado River by the Metropolitan Water District of Southern California (MWD). The source water for our wells is the Orange County Groundwater Basin that is replenished with water from the Santa Ana River, local runoff, imported water, and purified recycled water.  
  

## Imported Water
  
MWD's imported water supplies come from Northern California via the State Water Project (SWP) and from the Colorado River via Colorado River Aqueduct (CRA). MWD treats the raw water obtained from these sources at the Robert B. Diemer Filtration Plant located north of Yorba Linda and delivers it to Anaheim and other cities in Orange County through five feeders. Anaheim receives treated water from seven connections on MWD’s treated water feeders and one connection on a raw water feeder. Raw MWD water is delivered to Walnut Canyon Reservoir (WCR) and treated at Anaheim’s August F. Lenain Water Treatment Plant (Lenain) before entering the distribution system. The total available capacity is approximately 170 cubic feet per second (cfs).  

##    
**Orange County Groundwater Basin**
  
Managed by the Orange County Water District (OCWD), the groundwater basin is 350 square miles in area and lies beneath most of northern and central Orange County. Anaheim and more than 20 cities and retail water districts pump from the groundwater basin to provide water to homes and businesses. Your water source depends on where you live or work within the boundaries of our community. Generally, the source of water for areas east and south of the 57 and 91 freeway interchange is imported water. The central and western portions of Anaheim mostly receive groundwater or a blend with imported supplies.  
  

## **Groundwater Replenishment System**
  
In an effort to create a more reliable and local resource, the City is participating in the Groundwater Replenishment System.  
  
The Groundwater Replenishment System (GWRS) was constructed as a joint project of OCWD and the Orange County Sanitation District to recycle wastewater and provide a drought-proof source of water for our region.  
  
The GWRS uses advanced treatment technologies to clean wastewater and then returns it to groundwater via recharge basins in Anaheim and direct injection near the coast. The water is further cleaned by layers of soil and rock, and ultimately refills local water aquifers over time. It is important to remember that GWRS water does not go directly into your drinking water. Instead, it goes into the ground, where it will be naturally filtered by thousands of feet of soil and it will remain in the ground for years before it reaches a drinking water well.[website](https://www.anaheim.net/784/www.gwrsystem.com) or by calling OCWD directly at 714-378-3333. 
### Contact Us

Phone: 714-378-3333  
  
[Groundwater Replenishment System Website](http://www.gwrsystem.com/)

#  Environmental 
Below is a listing of current environmental documents online for public review. Please select the project (below) for the additional information.
You can also access online [Public Utilities: Environmental Documents](https://www.anaheim.net/507/Current-Environmental-Review).
**Project Name** | **Notices** | **Project Location, Description, & Environmental Documents** | **Contact**  
---|---|---|---  
**DEV2024-00058 Wyndham Hotel Density Modification** |  [Notice of Determination (NOD) ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/70043/) [2-27-2026 ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/70043/)   
[Notice of Determination (NOD) ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/70046/) [3-5-2026](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/70046/)   
[Notice of Intent (NOI) to Adopt a Mitigated Negative Declaration (MND) and Notice of Public Hearing](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/67333/) |  **PROJECT LOCATION** : 515 West Katella Avenue. The approximately 0.97-acre project site (APN 082-271-05) is at 515 West Katella Avenue in the central part of the City of Anaheim in Orange County. Regional access to the project site is from Interstate 5 (I-5), approximately 0.6 mile to the east via West Katella Avenue, and from State Route 22 (SR-22), approximately 2.5 miles to the south via South Harbor Boulevard.   
**PROJECT DESCRIPTION** : The applicant is proposing a General Plan Amendment and Specific Plan Amendment to the Anaheim Resort Specific Plan, to create a new density category for the project site, “Medium Density (Modified B)” to allow for up to 107 hotel rooms and 6,394 square feet of accessory uses. The original Wyndham Hotel building plans were designed with several dual suites that included two bedrooms and two bathrooms with internal connecting doors and one keyed entry door. Following the City’s approval of the project, additional entry doors were added to the dual suite rooms to create two separate keyed rooms. Some dual suites also had their interior connecting doors removed. This resulted in an increase in the total number of rooms from 72 to 107. Changes were also made to the use of space on the first floor without the addition of any square footage. Space that was previously approved for lobby guest areas, storage, and offices were converted to additional conference and meeting rooms and a restaurant that is open to the public (an increase in 6,394 SF of accessory structures). These were all changes made to the interior of the building; the overall development footprint of the building and remainder of the site did not change.   
[**Initial Study (IS)**](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/66808/) [• Appendix A - Air Quality and Greenhouse gas Analysis ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/66812/) [• Appendix B - Traffic Circulation Study ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/66811/) [• Appendix C - Noise Data ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/66810/) [• Appendix D - Native American Tribal Consultation Letters](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/66809/)   
|  Amanda Lauffer   
Phone: 714-765-4479    
  
**OC River Walk Project** |  [Notice of Determination 12-11-2025](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/70081/)   
[Notice of Availability (NOA)](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/65765/) |  **PROJECT LOCATION** : The project site is located along the Santa Ana River corridor, generally extending from Orangewood Avenue north to Ball Road and the existing Anaheim Coves at Burris Basin. It generally encompasses approximately a 2-mile stretch of the Santa Ana River, covering approximately 111 acres. It is approximately 15 miles upstream from the Pacific Ocean and adjacent to Angel Stadium, Anaheim Regional Transportation Intermodal Center (ARTIC), the Honda Center, and the Arena Corporate Center (ACC) buildings on the west side in the City of Anaheim. The ARTIC, the Honda Center, and the ACC buildings are all part of the OCVIBE Master Site Plan Project (OCVIBE Project). On the east side of the Santa Ana River, in the City of Orange, the project is near the Sandra Hutchens Regional Law Enforcement Training Center (a facility that includes a combat shooting range), the Orange County Public Works offices, the Stadium Promenade shopping mall, the Extended Stay America – Orange, and the Kinder Morgan Orange Terminal.   
**PROJECT DESCRIPTION** : The OC River Walk Conceptual Master Plan consists of various multi-purpose improvements to transform the Santa Ana River corridor and portions of the existing Santa Ana River Trail (SART), along with related off-site improvements necessary for its implementation. The main components of the Conceptual Master Plan involve creating two water impoundments by installing three inflatable rubber dams, modifying the riverbanks, constructing two pedestrian/bike bridges, providing new trails, improving existing trails, creating new and protection of existing access ramps, constructing two new undercrossings on the west bank at the Union Pacific Railroad and the Metrolink railroad, providing enhanced community recreation amenities (e.g., terraced seating, nature play areas, parklets, plazas, gathering areas for large-scale events, festivals, and other activities, water access, kayak launch, etc.), and other improvements.   
**Final Environmental Impact Report** **(EIR) No. 357** [• Findings of Fact ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/67277/) [• Response to Comments ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/67086/) [• Full Document (without appendices) ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/67076/)

**DEV2023-00043 Anaheim Hills Festival General Plan and Specific Plan Amendment** |  [Notice of Determination (NOD) ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/70222/) [3-25-2026](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/70222/)   
[Notice of Determination (NOD) ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/70045/) [3-5-2026](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/70045/)   
[Notice of Availability (NOA) ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/67334/)   
[Notice of Preparation (NOP)](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/53193/)   
[Scoping Meeting Presentation ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/53415/) [(February 2, 2024) ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/53415/)   
[Festival Anaheim Hills DEIR Planning Commission Workshop ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/65697/) [(July 14, 2025) ](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/65697/)   
|  **PROJECT LOCATION** : The project site is in the Anaheim Hills area of the City in Orange County, California. The Anaheim Hills area is predominately residential with commercial, and office uses along major corridors. The project site is the existing 85.7-acre Anaheim Hills Festival Specific Plan (Specific Plan), which includes the following Assessor Parcel Numbers (APNs): 354-451-01, -03, -04, -06, -08, -10, -12, -13, -17, -19, -20, -24, -27, -28, -31, -32, -33, -34, -35, -37, and -38. The Specific Plan, inclusive of the project’s development area, is located south of State Route 91 (SR 91) and is generally located south of Santa Ana Canyon Road, and west of Roosevelt Road.   
**PROJECT DESCRIPTION** : The project site’s proposed development area falls within the newly created DA 5 for Mixed-Use purposes. The development area is limited to DA 5, which includes the following APNs: 354-451-19 and -32. The exterior boundaries of the Specific Plan area would not change. The proposed development would demolish the existing approximately 62,676 square foot theater and, in its place, allow for the development of a 447-unit multi-family rental residential community. The proposed multi-family rental residential community would consist of a four-story multi-family residential building wrapped around a five-level parking structure with one subterranean level. As part of the project’s requested entitlements, a General Plan Amendment and Specific Plan Amendment would be required to allow for the proposed residential uses and for the creation of the new DA 5. The General Plan Amendment would change the land use designation from Regional Commercial to the Mixed-Use Medium for DA 5. The Specific Plan Amendment would amend the current Specific Plan to allow for mixed-use land uses on a total of 16.2 acres (DA 5) and would include other clarifying text amendments related to signage, permitted uses, etc.
